title: The Hour Before War: Trump Pauses Iran Strike as Gulf Allies Intervene
subhead: President Trump revealed he was one hour from ordering fresh strikes on Iran before Gulf states persuaded him to pause. Tehran has submitted a new peace proposal, but the gap between US demands and Iranian terms remains wide.

sections.heading: The Brinkmanship Pause
sections.body:
President Trump stated publicly on Tuesday that he had been 'an hour away' from ordering a new round of strikes on Iran before postponing the attack at the request of Gulf allies. The disclosure reveals just how close the region came to a major escalation. Trump simultaneously warned that the US may still need to strike again, while claiming Tehran is 'begging for a deal.'

Oil prices dropped more than 1% on the news, but the relief may prove temporary. The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve drew a record 9.9 million barrels last week, pushing total reserves to 374 million barrels — the lowest since July 2024. The drawdown reflects both the physical disruption from the Hormuz crisis and hedging against future supply shocks.
sections.whyItMatters: The pause demonstrates that Gulf states retain meaningful leverage over US decision-making, but the underlying standoff remains unresolved. Trump's willingness to broadcast how close he came to attack suggests a strategy of maximum pressure — and the next pause may not materialize.

sections.heading: Iran's New Proposal and the Sticking Points
sections.body:
Tehran has sent a new peace proposal to Washington, though its terms remain distant from US demands. According to sources, Iran's proposal includes demands for war reparations and the complete withdrawal of US troops from the region. Washington, by contrast, wants Iran to halt uranium enrichment for 20 years and ship out its existing stockpiles to the United States.

Neither side is publicly bending. The economic pressure on Iran has intensified, but Tehran appears to be betting that Washington's appetite for prolonged conflict remains limited — especially with oil markets already volatile and Gulf allies pressing for de-escalation.
sections.whyItMatters: The core conflict is structural: Washington wants guarantees that Iran cannot quickly rebuild nuclear capabilities; Tehran wants an end to US military presence in its neighborhood. Neither demand is easily conceded, and the middle ground remains undefined.

sections.heading: Putin's Beijing Visit: The Weakened Partner
sections.body:
Vladimir Putin arrived in Beijing on Tuesday for a two-day visit with Xi Jinping — his first major overseas trip since the Iran war escalated. The optics are deliberate: Putin follows Donald Trump's high-profile Beijing summit by less than a week, and Xi is receiving both leaders as China projects stability amid global turbulence.

But the power dynamics have shifted. The New York Times analysis frames Putin as a 'weakened' leader now deeply 'in Xi's pocket.' Russia is seeking to deepen energy ties with China as Persian Gulf disruptions create opportunities for alternative suppliers. The Russian ruble hit a three-year high against the yuan ahead of the visit, reflecting increased trade flows. Yet Xi also reportedly warned Trump last week that Putin may come to regret his Ukraine invasion — a signal that Beijing is hedging its Russia bets.
sections.whyItMatters: China is positioning itself as the indispensable power — engaging both Washington and Moscow while maintaining optionality. Putin needs Chinese economic support more than ever, but Xi has little incentive to overcommit to a partner whose military position in Ukraine remains strained.

sections.heading: The Trump-Putin Alaska Meeting
sections.body:
Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin are scheduled to meet face-to-face in Alaska on Friday. The agenda centers on finding pathways to end the war in Ukraine. The meeting follows weeks of diplomatic signaling, including Putin's recent peace overtures and Trump's public statements suggesting he wants to resolve the conflict.

The Alaska venue is notable — neutral ground, far from either capital, echoing Cold War summit traditions. But the context is different: Putin arrives from Beijing in a dependent position, while Trump comes from a Middle East crisis where he has demonstrated willingness to use force. The power balance at the table has shifted.
sections.whyItMatters: A serious negotiation on Ukraine would require concessions from both sides. Putin may be calculating that Trump's desire for a deal — combined with the distraction of the Iran crisis — creates an opening. But Trump also needs to avoid appearing to reward Russian aggression while simultaneously pressing Iran.

sections.heading: Economic Shockwaves Spread
sections.body:
Japan reported stronger-than-expected Q1 GDP growth on solid exports and consumption — but the data already feels like ancient history. The Iran war's energy shock has yet to fully filter through global supply chains. Japan, as a major energy importer, faces particular exposure. Analysts expect Q2 to show the real impact of sustained high oil prices and disrupted shipping routes.

In the US, incoming Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh has unsettled central banking peers by suggesting that Fed independence may not fully extend to its crisis-fighting role abroad. The implication — that the Fed might scale back its global dollar liquidity operations — has raised concerns about market stability precisely when the global financial system faces stress from energy price spikes and trade disruptions.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran conflict is no longer a regional story. Energy shocks, supply chain disruptions, and policy uncertainty are propagating through the global economy. The combination of military brinkmanship in the Gulf and central bank policy shifts in Washington creates multiple channels for financial instability.

sections.heading: Qatar's Diplomatic Position
sections.body:
Qatar publicly stated that no country has the right to block the Strait of Hormuz — a notable position from a state that has historically maintained good relations with Tehran. The statement reflects Gulf calculations that the costs of prolonged closure now outweigh any benefit of tacit support for Iranian actions. Qatar, along with other Gulf states, played a role in persuading Trump to pause the planned strike.

The Gulf states are walking a narrow line: pressing Washington for de-escalation while also signaling to Tehran that their tolerance for regional disruption has limits. Their leverage comes from their role as alternative energy suppliers and as hosts for US military bases.
sections.whyItMatters: Gulf diplomacy is now a live variable in the Iran equation. These states are not passive observers — they are actively shaping US and Iranian calculations through both public statements and private interventions.

bottomLine: The Iran standoff entered its most dangerous phase today: Trump was one hour from a major strike before Gulf allies secured a pause. Tehran has submitted a new proposal, but the gap between US demands (20-year enrichment halt, stockpile transfer) and Iranian terms (reparations, troop withdrawal) remains vast. Meanwhile, Putin visits Beijing in a weakened position before his Alaska summit with Trump on Friday, and economic shockwaves from the Gulf crisis are beginning to register across Asia and in central bank policy debates. The war has not ended — it has been paused, with the clock still running.
